text: Abel turned over to lay on his other side for the twelfth time that day, his mind and world having shrunken to the confines of the room.

He tried to escape his fatigue -- a special fatigue he had spent the last seven years frantically trying to outrun -- through sleep. But he had spent too much time sleeping, so had no recourse but to imitate it.
ASMR videos, weed, whiskey, porn... he'd burned through his mood-altering reserves and could not rely on those flaky friends either.
He was all alone with his newfound unemployment.
No... no. He couldn't face it. Not his own mind. Not the continued rumination on the future. He had to talk to someone. Anyone to keep himself out of that headspace.
There was his friend. The only one who was off work at this time, on the east coast.

sequence.speaker: Abel
text: Hey, odd question but can I rubber duck my future career with you some time soon?

sequence.speaker: Friend
text: Sure. I'm free in half an hour.

sequence.speaker: Abel
text: Perf. I've got nothing but time.
Half an hour passed. Abel hadn't moved.

sequence.speaker: Friend
text: Okay, so what are you thinking?

sequence.speaker: Abel
text: I don't know, man. Something physical. Something that gets me out of my own head. Well-paying manual jobs... like oil, or...

sequence.speaker: Friend
text: Fishing?

sequence.speaker: Abel
text: ...fishing. Yeah actually, fishing.

sequence.speaker: Friend
text: My cousin did a summer on the water up north. Said it changed his life. There's these coastal villages that take on apprentices.

sequence.speaker: Abel
text: Apprentices? Like, they actually teach you?

sequence.speaker: Friend
text: Yeah. Room, board, and a boat. You just have to show up and not drown.
For the first time in weeks, something stirred behind Abel's eyes.

sequence.speaker: Abel
text: Send me the link.
Two weeks later, Abel stepped off a bus at the edge of a small fishing village. The salt air hit him like a wall.
He had never been so far from home, or so close to something that felt like a beginning.
